subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Look around you right now. The cup you drink from, the sufuria your mother cooks bariis in, the water in the clay pot keeping cool in the shade, the air you are breathing this very moment - every single one of these is made of something we call a material. A material is simply what an object is made from. Your school shoes are made of rubber and cloth. A window is made of glass. A cooking pot might be made of metal or clay. In this unit you will become a materials detective, learning to look closely at the world and ask, 'What is this made of, and why was that material chosen?'
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Science 0097 — Stage 4 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Name and describe many properties of materials, such as hardness, flexibility, strength, transparency, whether they are waterproof, and whether they carry heat well.
Explain that every material is made of tiny particles that are too small to see.
Describe the three states of matter - solid, liquid and gas - and tell how the particles are arranged in each one.
Explain how heating and cooling can change a material from one state to another, for example ice melting into water and water boiling into steam.
Use the particle model to explain why solids keep their shape, why liquids flow and pour, and why gases spread out to fill a room.
Sort and group materials into natural materials (from nature) and manufactured materials (made by people).
Choose a suitable material for a job by matching the material's properties to what the job needs, such as choosing metal for a cooking pot or plastic for a rain cover.
Design a fair test to compare materials and record what you find in a clear table.
Here are the important words for this unit. Do not try to memorise them all at once. Come back to this table whenever you meet a word you have forgotten - that is exactly what a good scientist does.
What an object is made from.

concepts.title: Materials Are All Around You
concepts.explanation: Let us start with something simple. Pick up any object near you. Ask yourself two questions: 'What is it made of?' and 'Why was that material chosen?' Try it with a spoon. If it is a metal spoon, it is made of metal because metal is strong, does not break easily, and can be washed again and again. If it is a wooden spoon, it is made of wood because wood does not get too hot to hold when you stir hot food. Already you can see that people choose materials on purpose, matching the material to the job. Many objects…
concepts.example: Scientists like to sort materials into groups. One useful way is to ask, 'Did this come straight from nature, or did people make it?' Natural materials come from nature. Wood comes from trees such as the acacia or the…

concepts.title: Properties of Materials
concepts.explanation: A property is a feature of a material that you can observe or measure. Properties are the reason we choose one material over another. Let us meet the most important properties one by one, with examples from daily life. Hardness - how well a material resists being scratched or dented. A stone is hard; a sponge is soft. A metal knife is hard enough to cut, while soft butter is not.
concepts.example: Strength - how much force a material can take before it breaks. A thick rope is strong; a single thread is weak. Builders need strong materials for walls and roofs.

concepts.title: The Particle Model: The Hidden World Inside Everything
concepts.explanation: Now we come to one of the greatest ideas in science. Every material - solid, liquid or gas - is made of unimaginably tiny pieces called particles. They are so small that millions upon millions of them would fit onto the tip of a needle. You cannot see a single particle even with a school magnifying glass. Yet scientists are completely sure they are there, because thinking about particles explains so much about how materials behave. Here is a helpful way to picture it. Imagine a big pile of dry rice grains, or a…
concepts.example: The two big questions the particle model helps us answer are: How closely are the particles packed together? and How much do the particles move? The answers to these two questions are different for solids, liquids and…

concepts.title: The Three States of Matter
concepts.explanation: The three states of matter are solid, liquid and gas. The very same substance can exist in all three states. Water is the perfect example, and you can find all three in your own kitchen: ice is solid water, the water you drink is liquid water, and the steam rising from a boiling sufuria is water as a gas. Let us understand each state using our particle glasses. In a solid, the particles are packed very closely together, touching each other like people squeezed tightly in a crowded bus. They are held in fixed…
concepts.example: This is why you cannot pour a stone or a block of wood. The particles are locked together, so the whole object moves as one piece. Examples of solids all around you include wood, metal, glass, rock, ice, plastic, bone…

concepts.title: Comparing the Three States
concepts.explanation: Scientists love to compare things in a neat table so the differences jump out. Here is a comparison of the three states. Read across each row and notice how the properties change as the particles get further apart and move more freely.
concepts.example: Scientists love to compare things in a neat table so the differences jump out. Here is a comparison of the three states. Read across each row and notice how the properties change as the particles get further apart and…

concepts.title: Changing State: Heating and Cooling
concepts.explanation: One of the most exciting things about materials is that they can change from one state to another. A solid can become a liquid, a liquid can become a gas, and it can all happen in reverse too. What causes these changes? The answer is heat energy. Heating adds energy and makes particles move faster; cooling removes energy and makes particles slow down. Let us follow water as it changes, because you can watch every step of this in your own home on the charcoal stove. Take an ice cube out of the freezer and leave it…
concepts.example: Now do the opposite. Put liquid water into the freezer. As it cools, the particles lose energy and move more slowly, until they can no longer slide around and they lock into fixed places. The liquid has become a solid.…

explorations.title: Watching Ice Melt (Solid to Liquid)
explorations.context: To watch a solid change into a liquid, and to see how warmth speeds up melting.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, write down what you think: Which ice cube will melt faster - one left in a warm sunny spot or one left in a cool shady spot? Why?
explorations.answer: Which cube melted faster? Was your hypothesis correct? What was the ice giving the particles that made them break free and slide - was it heat or cold? Explain, using particles, why warmth makes ice melt faster.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To watch a solid change into a liquid, and to see how warmth speeds up melting.

explorations.title: The Disappearing Water (Evaporation)
explorations.context: To watch a liquid slowly change into a gas, and to find out what makes evaporation faster.
explorations.prompt: Write your prediction: Will water dry up faster in a warm sunny place or in a cool shady place?
explorations.answer: Which saucer dried up first? Was your hypothesis correct? Where did the water go? It did not disappear - what did it become? Why does heat make water evaporate faster? Answer using particles.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To watch a liquid slowly change into a gas, and to find out what makes evaporation faster.

explorations.title: Water Drops from Nowhere (Condensation)
explorations.context: To watch a gas turn back into a liquid, showing that evaporation can be reversed by cooling.
explorations.prompt: Predict: What will appear on the outside of a cold glass left in warm air? Where will it come from?
explorations.answer: The outside of the glass was dry and you did not pour any water on it. So where did the water drops come from? The warm air holds invisible water vapour. What did the cold glass do to that vapour? Is condensation the opposite of evaporation? Explain your answer.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To watch a gas turn back into a liquid, showing that evaporation can be reversed by cooling.

explorations.title: Testing Materials for Waterproofing
explorations.context: To carry out a fair test to find which household materials are waterproof and which are absorbent.
explorations.prompt: Predict which of your materials will keep water out and which will soak it up, and give a reason.
explorations.answer: Which materials were waterproof? Which were absorbent? Which material would be best for making a rain cover, and why? Which material would be best for making a towel or a cleaning cloth, and why? Why was it important to drip the same amount of water on each material?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To carry out a fair test to find which household materials are waterproof and which are absorbent.

explorations.title: Which Materials Carry Heat? (Conductors and Insulators)
explorations.context: To find out which household materials are good heat conductors and which are insulators.
explorations.prompt: Predict: When you stand a metal spoon and a wooden spoon in warm water, which handle will warm up first?
explorations.answer: Which spoon handle warmed up fastest? Which stayed coolest? Which materials are good heat conductors? Which are insulators? Why does your mother use a cloth or a wooden-handled tool to lift a hot metal sufuria from the jiko?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out which household materials are good heat conductors and which are insulators.

methods.title: Watching Ice Melt (Solid to Liquid)
methods.example: To watch a solid change into a liquid, and to see how warmth speeds up melting.
steps: Place one ice cube on each plate.
Put one plate in a warm, sunny place (such as near a window) and the other in a cool, shady place.
Write down the start time.
Every five minutes, look at both cubes and write down what you see.
Record the time when each cube has completely melted into a puddle of water.

methods.title: The Disappearing Water (Evaporation)
methods.example: To watch a liquid slowly change into a gas, and to find out what makes evaporation faster.
steps: Pour the same small amount of water into each saucer.
Place one saucer in a warm sunny spot and the other in a cool shady spot.
Check them once in the morning and once in the evening for two or three days.
Each time, record how much water is left in each saucer.
Note which saucer becomes empty first.

methods.title: Water Drops from Nowhere (Condensation)
methods.example: To watch a gas turn back into a liquid, showing that evaporation can be reversed by cooling.
steps: Fill the glass with cold water and add a couple of ice cubes to make it very cold.
Wipe the outside of the glass dry with the cloth.
Leave the glass on a table in a warm room for ten minutes.
Look closely at the outside of the glass and touch it gently.
Record what you see and feel on the outside surface.

methods.title: Testing Materials for Waterproofing
methods.example: To carry out a fair test to find which household materials are waterproof and which are absorbent.
steps: Lay each material square flat on a table.
To keep the test fair, drip the same number of water drops onto each material from the same height.
Wait one minute.
Look at each material: did the water sit on top (waterproof) or soak in (absorbent)?
Record your result for each material.

methods.title: Which Materials Carry Heat? (Conductors and Insulators)
methods.example: To find out which household materials are good heat conductors and which are insulators.
steps: Ask an adult to help you pour warm (not boiling) water into a cup.
Stand the metal spoon and the wooden spoon in the water at the same time, handles sticking up.
After two minutes, carefully touch the top of each handle with your fingers.
Record which handle feels warmer.
If you have a plastic spoon, test it the same way and compare.

workedExamples.title: Reading a Melting Table
workedExamples.prompt: In science we often measure things and write them in a table. Here is data from a pretend investigation where Amina put an ice cube on a plate in a warm room and measured its temperature every two minutes with a thermometer. Let us read the table together and…
workedExamples.solution: Time (minutes) Temperature (degrees C) What Amina saw

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: All materials are made of: a) tiny particles b) water c) air d) metal
workedExamples.solution: 2 a) tiny particles Every material is made of tiny particles far too small to see.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: In which state are the particles packed closest together? a) gas b) liquid c) solid d) they are all the same
workedExamples.solution: 3 c) solid In a solid the particles are packed closest together and touching, held in fixed places.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: What is the change from a solid to a liquid called? a) freezing b) melting c) evaporation d) condensation
workedExamples.solution: 4 b) melting Melting is solid turning to liquid as it warms and particles break free to slide.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: What is the change from a liquid to a gas called? a) melting b) freezing c) evaporation d) condensation
workedExamples.solution: 5 c) evaporation Evaporation is a liquid changing into a gas as fast particles escape the surface.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Which material is a good heat conductor? a) wood b) cloth c) metal d) plastic
workedExamples.solution: 6 c) metal Metal lets heat pass through quickly, so it is a good heat conductor.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: Which property makes glass a good choice for a window? a) it is soft b) it is transparent c) it is absorbent d) it is bendy
workedExamples.solution: 7 b) it is transparent Transparent glass lets light through so you can see clearly and let daylight in.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is a natural material? a) plastic b) glass c) wood d) steel
workedExamples.solution: 8 c) wood Wood comes from trees, so it is natural. Plastic, glass and steel are manufactured.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a state of matter? a) metal b) liquid c) hardness d) glass
practice.answer: 1 b) liquid Solid, liquid and gas are the three states of matter. Metal and glass are materials; hardness is a property.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: All materials are made of: a) tiny particles b) water c) air d) metal
practice.answer: 2 a) tiny particles Every material is made of tiny particles far too small to see.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: In which state are the particles packed closest together? a) gas b) liquid c) solid d) they are all the same
practice.answer: 3 c) solid In a solid the particles are packed closest together and touching, held in fixed places.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: What is the change from a solid to a liquid called? a) freezing b) melting c) evaporation d) condensation
practice.answer: 4 b) melting Melting is solid turning to liquid as it warms and particles break free to slide.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: What is the change from a liquid to a gas called? a) melting b) freezing c) evaporation d) condensation
practice.answer: 5 c) evaporation Evaporation is a liquid changing into a gas as fast particles escape the surface.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which material is a good heat conductor? a) wood b) cloth c) metal d) plastic
practice.answer: 6 c) metal Metal lets heat pass through quickly, so it is a good heat conductor.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which property makes glass a good choice for a window? a) it is soft b) it is transparent c) it is absorbent d) it is bendy
practice.answer: 7 b) it is transparent Transparent glass lets light through so you can see clearly and let daylight in.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a natural material? a) plastic b) glass c) wood d) steel
practice.answer: 8 c) wood Wood comes from trees, so it is natural. Plastic, glass and steel are manufactured.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which material would be best for a rain cover? a) paper b) cloth c) plastic sheet d) cardboard
practice.answer: 9 c) plastic sheet Plastic is waterproof and light, so water cannot pass through it.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: When steam cools on a cold spoon and turns to water drops, this is called: a) melting b) condensation c) freezing d) evaporation
practice.answer: 10 b) condensation A gas cooling and turning back into a liquid is condensation.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Why can you pour water but not a stone? a) water is heavier b) water particles can slide around each other c) the stone is bigger d) water has no particles
practice.answer: 11 b) water particles can slide around each other In a liquid the particles slide past each other, so it flows and pours.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: What happens to particles when you heat a material? a) they stop moving b) they move faster c) they disappear d) they get bigger
practice.answer: 12 b) they move faster Heating adds energy, so particles vibrate or move faster.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Watching Ice Melt (Solid to Liquid)
activities.materials: Two ice cubes of the same size from the freezer; Two small plates or saucers; A clock or phone to tell the time; Your notebook and a pen
steps: Place one ice cube on each plate.
Put one plate in a warm, sunny place (such as near a window) and the other in a cool, shady place.
Write down the start time.
Every five minutes, look at both cubes and write down what you see.
Record the time when each cube has completely melted into a puddle of water.

activities.title: The Disappearing Water (Evaporation)
activities.materials: Two identical small saucers or lids; The same small amount of water for each (a few spoonfuls); A pen to mark the water level; Your notebook
steps: Pour the same small amount of water into each saucer.
Place one saucer in a warm sunny spot and the other in a cool shady spot.
Check them once in the morning and once in the evening for two or three days.
Each time, record how much water is left in each saucer.
Note which saucer becomes empty first.

activities.title: Water Drops from Nowhere (Condensation)
activities.materials: A clean drinking glass; Cold water and a few ice cubes from the freezer; A dry cloth to wipe up; Your notebook
steps: Fill the glass with cold water and add a couple of ice cubes to make it very cold.
Wipe the outside of the glass dry with the cloth.
Leave the glass on a table in a warm room for ten minutes.
Look closely at the outside of the glass and touch it gently.
Record what you see and feel on the outside surface.

activities.title: Testing Materials for Waterproofing
activities.materials: Small squares of different materials: a plastic bag piece, cloth, paper, foil, a leaf; A spoon of water; A dropper or just your fingers to drip water; Your notebook
steps: Lay each material square flat on a table.
To keep the test fair, drip the same number of water drops onto each material from the same height.
Wait one minute.
Look at each material: did the water sit on top (waterproof) or soak in (absorbent)?
Record your result for each material.

activities.title: Which Materials Carry Heat? (Conductors and Insulators)
activities.materials: A metal spoon and a wooden spoon (and a plastic spoon if you have one); A cup of warm - not boiling - water; Your hand to feel the handles; Your notebook
steps: Ask an adult to help you pour warm (not boiling) water into a cup.
Stand the metal spoon and the wooden spoon in the water at the same time, handles sticking up.
After two minutes, carefully touch the top of each handle with your fingers.
Record which handle feels warmer.
If you have a plastic spoon, test it the same way and compare.

activities.title: Observe: Changing State: Heating and Cooling
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of changing state: heating and cooling around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

terms: Material
What an object is made from (glass, wood, metal, plastic, clay).
Property
A feature of a material you can observe or measure (hard, bendy, waterproof).
Particle
A tiny piece of matter, far too small to see. Everything is made of particles.
Matter
Anything with weight that takes up space.
Solid
Keeps its own shape. Particles packed tight, vibrating on the spot.
Liquid
Flows and takes the container's shape. Particles close but sliding.
Gas
Fills any container. Particles far apart, flying around freely.
Melting
Solid to liquid, caused by heating.
Freezing
Liquid to solid, caused by cooling.
Evaporation
Liquid to gas, as fast particles escape the surface.
Condensation
Gas to liquid, when a gas is cooled.
Natural material
Comes from nature (wood, cotton, stone, clay).

vocabulary.meaning: What an object is made from (glass, wood, metal, plastic, clay).
vocabulary.example: The cup you drink from, the sufuria your mother cooks bariis in, the water in the clay pot keeping cool in the shade, the air you are breathing this very moment - every single one of these is made of something we call a…

vocabulary.meaning: A feature of a material you can observe or measure (hard, bendy, waterproof).
vocabulary.example: A property is a feature of a material that you can observe or measure.

vocabulary.meaning: A tiny piece of matter, far too small to see. Everything is made of particles.
vocabulary.example: Use the particle model to explain why solids keep their shape, why liquids flow and pour, and why gases spread out to fill a room.

vocabulary.meaning: Anything with weight that takes up space.
vocabulary.example: Describe the three states of matter - solid, liquid and gas - and tell how the particles are arranged in each one.

vocabulary.meaning: Keeps its own shape. Particles packed tight, vibrating on the spot.
vocabulary.example: The way water can be a hard solid, a flowing liquid, and an invisible gas - all from the same substance - is one of the countless wonders of creation worth thinking about.

vocabulary.meaning: Flows and takes the container's shape. Particles close but sliding.
vocabulary.example: The way water can be a hard solid, a flowing liquid, and an invisible gas - all from the same substance - is one of the countless wonders of creation worth thinking about.

vocabulary.meaning: Fills any container. Particles far apart, flying around freely.
vocabulary.example: The way water can be a hard solid, a flowing liquid, and an invisible gas - all from the same substance - is one of the countless wonders of creation worth thinking about.

vocabulary.meaning: Solid to liquid, caused by heating.
vocabulary.example: Explain how heating and cooling can change a material from one state to another, for example ice melting into water and water boiling into steam.

vocabulary.meaning: Liquid to solid, caused by cooling.
vocabulary.example: Freezing is simply the reverse of melting, and for water it happens at the same temperature, zero degrees Celsius.

vocabulary.meaning: Liquid to gas, as fast particles escape the surface.
vocabulary.example: Evaporation takes heat away, so the water left inside stays cool - a natural fridge with no electricity at all!

vocabulary.meaning: Gas to liquid, when a gas is cooled.
vocabulary.example: This change from gas to liquid is called condensation.

vocabulary.meaning: Comes from nature (wood, cotton, stone, clay).
vocabulary.example: Name one natural material and one manufactured material in your classroom, and say how you decided.

commonMistakes: You cannot see it, so it feels empty.
Thinking solid particles do not move.
Both make a solid seem to turn liquid.
Thinking the white cloud above a pot is steam.

connections.text: evaporation, condensation and rain all use the changes of state you learned here.
conductors and insulators explain how heat moves - you will meet these ideas again.
the same words conductor and insulator are used for electricity (metal conducts, plastic insulates), which keeps us safe with wires.
knowing about natural materials links to farming, rocks and how people use the land.
Everyday life and design technology: choosing materials is exactly what builders, cooks and makers do every day.
Watch salt appear as a saucer of salty water dries in the sun over several days.

realProblems.prompt: Use what you have learned to solve these real-life problems from daily life.
realProblems.answer: 1 She should use the clay pot. A little water seeps through the clay and evaporates on the outside. Evaporation takes heat away, cooling the water inside. A metal tin has no holes and conducts outside heat into the water, so it stays warm.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1 She should use the clay pot. A little water seeps through the clay and evaporates on the outside. Evaporation takes heat away, cooling the water inside. A metal tin has no holes and conducts outside heat into the water, so it stays warm.

realProblems.prompt: Halima wants to keep drinking water cool in the heat near Garissa without a fridge. Should she use a metal tin or a clay pot? Explain your choice using the science of evaporation.
realProblems.answer: 2 Metal sheeting (or strong tiles). Reasons: it is waterproof so rain cannot pass through, and it is strong and durable so it lasts a long time. (Accept other sensible strong, waterproof materials with reasons.)
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 2 Metal sheeting (or strong tiles). Reasons: it is waterproof so rain cannot pass through, and it is strong and durable so it lasts a long time. (Accept other sensible strong, waterproof materials with reasons.)

realProblems.prompt: A builder in Hargeisa needs a material for a roof that keeps out rain and lasts a long time. Suggest a suitable material and give two reasons using its properties.
realProblems.answer: 3 Heat and wind give the water particles more energy. The fastest particles at the surface escape into the air as water vapour (a gas). The wind carries them away so more can escape, and the shirt dries.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 3 Heat and wind give the water particles more energy. The fastest particles at the surface escape into the air as water vapour (a gas). The wind carries them away so more can escape, and the shirt dries.

realProblems.prompt: Yusuf leaves a wet shirt on the line in the hot, windy afternoon. Explain, using particles, why it dries.
realProblems.answer: 4 Glass. It is transparent, so it lets light through, but it is also hard and waterproof, so it keeps out dust and rain while you can still see through it.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 4 Glass. It is transparent, so it lets light through, but it is also hard and waterproof, so it keeps out dust and rain while you can still see through it.

realProblems.prompt: Amina's family wants a window that lets in light but keeps out dust and rain. Which material should they choose, and why?
realProblems.answer: 5 Metal is a good heat conductor, so it quickly carries the soup's heat up the spoon to your hand. Wood is an insulator, so it carries heat slowly and the handle stays cool enough to hold.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 5 Metal is a good heat conductor, so it quickly carries the soup's heat up the spoon to your hand. Wood is an insulator, so it carries heat slowly and the handle stays cool enough to hold.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name three properties you can use to describe a material.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1 Any three of: hardness, strength, flexibility, transparency, waterproof, heat conductivity, colour, density. Each is a feature you can observe or measure.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What are all materials made of?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 2 All materials are made of tiny particles that are too small to see, even with a magnifying glass.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Describe how the particles are arranged in a solid.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 3 In a solid the particles are packed very closely together in fixed places. They can only vibrate on the spot, which is why a solid keeps its shape.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain why a gas spreads out to fill its whole container.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 4 A gas spreads out because its particles are far apart and fly around freely in all directions, so they move out to fill the whole container.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the difference between a natural material and a manufactured material? Give one example of each.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 5 A natural material comes from nature (example: wood or cotton). A manufactured material is made or changed by people (example: plastic or glass). Accept any correct examples.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain what happens to the particles in ice when it melts.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 6 The ice particles gain heat energy, vibrate harder and harder, then break free from their fixed places and begin to slide around each other. The solid has become a liquid.

questions.question: Which of these is a state of matter?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: liquid Solid, liquid and gas are the three states of matter. Metal and glass are materials; hardness is a property.

questions.question: All materials are made of:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: tiny particles Every material is made of tiny particles far too small to see.

questions.question: In which state are the particles packed closest together?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: solid In a solid the particles are packed closest together and touching, held in fixed places.

questions.question: What is the change from a solid to a liquid called?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: melting Melting is solid turning to liquid as it warms and particles break free to slide.

questions.question: What is the change from a liquid to a gas called?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: evaporation Evaporation is a liquid changing into a gas as fast particles escape the surface.

questions.question: Which material is a good heat conductor?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: metal Metal lets heat pass through quickly, so it is a good heat conductor.

questions.question: Which property makes glass a good choice for a window?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: it is transparent Transparent glass lets light through so you can see clearly and let daylight in.

questions.question: Which of these is a natural material?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: wood Wood comes from trees, so it is natural. Plastic, glass and steel are manufactured.

questions.question: Which material would be best for a rain cover?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: plastic sheet Plastic is waterproof and light, so water cannot pass through it.

questions.question: When steam cools on a cold spoon and turns to water drops, this is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: condensation A gas cooling and turning back into a liquid is condensation.

questions.question: Why can you pour water but not a stone?
options: water particles can slide around each other
questions.answer: water particles can slide around each other
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: water particles can slide around each other In a liquid the particles slide past each other, so it flows and pours.

questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: they move faster Heating adds energy, so particles vibrate or move faster.

selfAssessment: I can name and describe many properties of materials, such as hardness, flexibility, strength, transparency, whether they are waterproof, and whether they carry heat well.
I can explain that every material is made of tiny particles that are too small to see.
I can describe the three states of matter - solid, liquid and gas - and tell how the particles are arranged in each one.
I can explain how heating and cooling can change a material from one state to another, for example ice melting into water and water boiling into steam.
I can use the particle model to explain why solids keep their shape, why liquids flow and pour, and why gases spread out to fill a room.
I can sort and group materials into natural materials (from nature) and manufactured materials (made by people).
I can choose a suitable material for a job by matching the material's properties to what the job needs, such as choosing metal for a cooking pot or plastic for a rain cover.
I can design a fair test to compare materials and record what you find in a clear table.
